title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 13
tldr: Web dev play of the day: add durable, crash-proof background jobs to any full-stack app with two directives. Vercel's Workflow SDK now runs natively in Nitro v3 (shipped today, Jun 13). Install workflow, add modules: ["workflow/nitro"], then mark a function "use workflow" and its sub-steps "use step" — each step is checkpointed, so the job survives crashes, timeouts and retries, and sleep("5s") suspends with zero compute cost. The reliable shape for onboarding emails, payment flows and AI pipelines, now a framework primitive (beta, deploys best on Vercel Fluid compute).

Graphic — stop screenshotting sites you admire; capture them as editable Figma layers. Figma's official Chrome extension (capture-to-layers, shipped Jun 11) brings any live webpage onto the canvas as real frames, text, images and vectors — not a flat image. Click the extension → Capture page or Select element, paste into Figma, and you can restyle the type, recolour, and reuse the layout as a starting point. The fast way to deconstruct a competitor's UI or pull a real-world pattern into a moodboard (beta, paid plans).

Email — add one purposeful micro-animation that survives Gmail's clip and Outlook. The durable recipe (Litmus): design the static version first, then layer one small GIF motion (a CTA pulse, a progress bar, a product-variation cycle) — keep total weight under ~1–1.5MB, stop the loop after 2–3 repeats, never strobe, and make sure the first frame stands alone (some clients strip the animation). Motion with a job, not decoration.

News — your production model can vanish overnight: the US government ordered Anthropic to pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5. Anthropic received an export-control directive Jun 12 (5:21pm ET) and disabled both models for all customers to comply; other Claude models are unaffected. Anthropic says it believes the order rests on a narrow jailbreak finding it considers a "misunderstanding" (and says the capability is widely available, incl. GPT-5.5) and is working to restore access. The do-it for us: never hard-wire one model — route AI calls through a gateway with a fallback model list so a yanked or down model fails over without a redeploy.

News — the AI-chip V-shape held into mid-week, and Marvell (not Nvidia) is the index event. After the Jun 3–6 rout, chip names rebounded the week of Jun 8 on reaffirmed ~$750B hyperscaler 2026 capex; the rebound carried through mid-week. The concrete catalyst is Marvell joining the S&P 500 on Jun 22 (S&P DJI confirmed Jun 5) — index funds become forced buyers. Directional as ever; the durable read is the V-shape + the why (a spend-scrutiny repricing, not a thesis break).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is own the seam, not just the tool — a workflow that checkpoints every step so a crash can't lose state, a fallback model list so one provider can't take your feature down, a static-first email so a stripped GIF still lands. The Fable 5 shutdown is the lesson in one headline: the capability you rent can disappear on someone else's order, so build the off-ramp before you need it.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: durable, resumable background jobs in any Nitro v3 app (Vercel Workflow SDK)

blocks.sub: Vercel's Workflow SDK now runs natively in Nitro v3 — mark a function "use workflow" and its steps "use step" and the job survives crashes, timeouts and retries
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 13, 2026): Vercel's Workflow SDK shipped a native Nitro v3 integration (beta). Previously workflow steps ran in separate bundles; now they execute inside the same bundled runtime as the rest of your app, so server APIs (e.g. Nitro's useStorage()) work directly inside a step, builds tree-shake properly, and any Nitro/Vite full-stack app — not just Next.js — gets durable execution. Why it matters for our builds: the jobs that actually break in production are the multi-step, long-running ones — onboarding sequences, payment/fulfilment flows, AI pipelines that call a model, wait, then call again. Hand-rolled, a server restart or a function timeout mid-sequence loses state and either drops the job or double-charges someone. A durable workflow checkpoints each step: if the process dies, it resumes from the last completed step rather than the top; a failed step auto-retries; and sleep("5s") (or hours/days) suspends the run without burning compute while it waits. It's the reliable planner→do→wait→do shape we keep teaching, now a two-directive primitive instead of a queue + state machine you maintain by hand.
blocks.doIt: (1) Install: npm i workflow. (2) Register the module in nitro.config.ts: export default defineConfig({ modules: ["workflow/nitro"] }). (3) Write the workflow — the orchestrator gets "use workflow", each unit of work gets "use step": export async function onboard(email){ "use workflow"; const user = await createUser(email); await sleep("5s"); await sendWelcome(user); } async function createUser(email){ "use step"; /* ... */ }. (4) Kick it off from a route with import { start } from "workflow/api" → await start(onboard, [email]). (5) Make steps idempotent (a retried step must not double-act) and throw FatalError for the genuinely-unrecoverable case so it skips retries instead of looping. (6) Deploy to Vercel with Fluid compute enabled — that's what powers the efficient suspend/resume.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: the Nitro v3 integration is beta, and Workflow apps currently "work best when deployed to Vercel" (Fluid compute does the suspend/resume) — it's not a portable, run-anywhere durable engine yet. The portable principle holds regardless: decompose long jobs into checkpointed, idempotent, retryable steps; never run a multi-step external sequence as one un-resumable function. Card: card-webdev-vercel-workflow-nitro. Logged a prediction (Nitro v3 integration reaches GA by Q3-end).

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: capture any live webpage as editable Figma layers (not a screenshot)

blocks.sub: Figma's official Chrome extension brings a webpage onto the canvas as real frames, text, images and vectors — restyle and riff on production UI instead of redrawing it
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 11, 2026): Figma shipped a Chrome extension that captures a webpage to editable layers. Point it at any live page, capture the whole page or a single element, paste into Figma Design — and every part arrives as a real Figma object (frame, text node, image, vector), not a flat PNG. Why it matters for our craft: the daily reality of design work is studying things that already exist — a competitor's pricing table, a SaaS dashboard's empty state, a checkout flow you want to better. Today that usually means a screenshot you can only trace over, or a slow manual rebuild. Capturing to layers collapses that: you get the real spacing, type scale, and component structure to deconstruct, recolour to a client's palette, swap the copy, and use as a jump-start for a redesign or a Figma Make prototype. It's the same 'pull real-world structure in as data, then riff' move we taught for AI image briefs — applied to live UI.
blocks.doIt: (1) Install the Figma Capture Chrome extension from the Chrome Web Store and sign in. (2) Open the page you want to study; click the extension in the toolbar. (3) Choose Capture page for the whole thing, or Select element → hover to highlight a region → click to grab just that component. (4) Switch to a Figma Design file and paste — the capture lands as editable layers. (5) Work it: recolour to the client's brand hexes (pair with Khroma — card-graphic-color-palette), swap fonts/copy, detach what you need, and keep it as a reference frame beside your redesign. (6) Treat it as a starting point, not a deliverable — clean up the layer tree and rebuild messy nodes as proper components.
blocks.note: Caveats: it's in beta on paid plans, and it captures the page's rendered state, so a complex site can come in as a deep, slightly messy layer tree you'll want to tidy. Don't ship someone else's design — use it to learn structure and accelerate your own. ('Generate designs using design systems' from a capture is flagged 'coming soon.') Card: card-graphic-figma-capture-layers.

sections.h: Email — run it today: one purposeful micro-animation that survives the inbox

blocks.sub: The static-first GIF recipe — add a single small motion (CTA pulse, progress bar, product-variation cycle) that degrades to a clean still in clients that strip it
blocks.p: What's the play: motion in email earns attention, but only when it's intentional and engineered for the inbox's constraints. The durable recipe (from Litmus's micro-animation guide) is static-first: design the still version that carries the whole message, then layer one small animated GIF on top for a single job — a CTA that pulses once or twice, a loyalty progress bar that fills, a product shot that cycles through its colour variants, an urgency cue on a time-limited offer. Why it matters for retention work: a subtle, well-placed motion lifts click-through on the element that matters without the fragility of AMP/interactive email (patchy client support). Because it's a GIF, the worst case in a client that strips animation is your deliberate first frame — not a broken layout.
blocks.doIt: (1) Design the static email first and confirm it fully works with no motion — that frame is your fallback. (2) Pick one element to animate with a clear function (CTA, progress bar, product variations, urgency). (3) Export a tight GIF: make the first frame the standalone static version, and stop the loop after 2–3 repeats (don't loop forever). (4) Watch weight: keep total asset weight under ~1–1.5MB, and know your baseline HTML size — Gmail clips the message at ~102KB, which can hide your footer/unsubscribe. (5) Place it above the fold (motion below the fold is wasted) and never use flashing/strobing content (photosensitive-seizure risk). (6) Render-test across Gmail/Outlook/Apple Mail (Litmus or Email on Acid) before sending, and confirm the static fallback looks intentional everywhere.
blocks.note: GIF, not CSS/AMP: this is the broadly-compatible path (Outlook and most clients honour a GIF's static frame). Reserve true interactivity (AMP carousels, in-email forms) for the few clients that support it, with a static fallback. Card: card-email-micro-animation.

sections.h: News — the landscape: Washington pulls Anthropic's top models offline, and the chip V-shape holds
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: US government orders Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the model you depend on can disappear on someone else's order
blocks.p: What happened: on Jun 12 (5:21pm ET) Anthropic received a US government export-control directive, citing national-security authority, to suspend access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national (inside or outside the US, including foreign-national Anthropic employees). Because that's impractical to enforce per-user in real time, the net effect is that Anthropic disabled both models for all customers to ensure compliance; all other Claude models are unaffected. Anthropic's stated understanding is that the government believes it found a method of jailbreaking Fable 5 — but Anthropic says it reviewed the report it believes is the basis, found the demonstrated capability is widely available from other frontier models (including OpenAI's GPT-5.5), called the directive a likely "misunderstanding," and said it is working to restore access. Why it matters to us: this is the data-governance/availability thread escalating from 'check the retention terms' to 'the capability itself can be switched off overnight by a third party.' For any team shipping a feature on a single top-tier model, that's pure continuity risk — and it's a vendor-neutral lesson, not an Anthropic problem.
blocks.note: Confirmed (≥3 independent): Anthropic's own statement plus CNBC, Bloomberg, NBC News, Fortune and Ars Technica. The jailbreak rationale is Anthropic's characterisation of the government's reasoning, not an official public finding — treat the 'why' as reported, and the situation as fast-moving (access may be restored). Advances the frontier-model-governance thread; logged a prediction on restoration.

blocks.sub: The do-it for us: route AI calls through a gateway with a fallback model list so a yanked (or down) model fails over without a redeploy
blocks.p: The practical takeaway from the Fable 5 shutdown is the same resilience pattern we apply to any third-party dependency: don't hard-wire one model. If your app or internal tool calls a single model ID directly and that model is pulled, deprecated, or rate-limited, your feature is down until someone ships a code change. A model gateway turns that into a config-level failover — you declare a priority list and the gateway tries the next model on error, so a vanished model degrades gracefully (to a slightly weaker or different-provider model) instead of breaking.
blocks.doIt: (1) Put your LLM calls behind a gateway/router (OpenRouter, Vercel AI Gateway, or Cloudflare AI Gateway) instead of a single provider SDK. (2) With OpenRouter, pass a models array in priority order (up to 3) — on an error it automatically tries the next, and you're billed for whichever actually ran. (3) Choose fallbacks that are genuinely independent — a different provider for the backup (e.g. a Claude primary with an OpenAI or open-weight fallback), so a single-vendor outage can't take out both. (4) Keep your prompts model-portable (avoid one model's proprietary quirks) and log which model served each request so you can see when you're silently on the backup. (5) Pair with a hard spend cap (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits) so the failover can't quietly route you to a pricier model unbudgeted.
blocks.note: Failover trades a little quality/consistency for uptime — validate that your backup model clears your task's quality bar before you rely on it, and alert when you fall back so a 'temporary' downgrade doesn't become permanent. Card: card-ai-tooling-model-portability (thread-govern-ai-spend); related to the Fable-5 retention card.

blocks.sub: Markets: the AI-chip V-shape held into mid-week; Marvell — not Nvidia — is the index catalyst
blocks.p: What happened: after the Jun 3–6 AI-chip rout (SOXX ~−10% on the week, Nvidia briefly under $5T), chip names rebounded the week of Jun 8 — Micron up ~+10% intraday off a −13% Friday, Nvidia and Broadcom higher — on reaffirmed hyperscaler 2026 capex (~$750B); that recovery carried through mid-week. The concrete, dated index event is Marvell Technology (MRVL) joining the S&P 500 on Jun 22 (S&P Dow Jones Indices confirmed Jun 5), which forces index funds to buy it. (Correction to our earlier note: the pending-S&P-500 catalyst is Marvell; Nvidia has long been an index constituent.) Why it matters to us: it reinforces the same climate read — the market is grading AI spend discipline, not abandoning the build — so keep leading client AI pitches with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling.
blocks.note: Directional — intraday percentages age fast (SOXX week-of-Jun-3 −10% and the Jun-8-week rebound corroborated across CNBC/Intellectia/Motley Fool; single-stock daily moves are snapshots). The durable facts are the V-shape + the why, and the Marvell inclusion date (S&P DJI, cross-checked vs Tech Times/Investing.com). The 'dip not regime change' call (due ~Jul 20) continues to lean held.

sources: Vercel Changelog — Workflow SDK now runs natively in Nitro v3 · Workflow SDK Docs — Getting started on Nitro · Vercel Docs — Workflows · Figma — Release notes (Jun 11, 2026: capture webpages as editable layers) · Figma Learn — Capture web pages to layers with the Chrome extension · Chrome Web Store — Figma Capture · Litmus — Micro Animations, Macro Impact: 10 use-cases you can try today · Anthropic — Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 · CNBC — Anthropic disables Fable 5 and Mythos 5 to comply with government directive · Bloomberg — US orders halt to foreign access for Fable 5, Mythos 5 · Ars Technica — Anthropic shuts down Fable, Mythos following government directive · OpenRouter — Model Fallbacks (automatic failover) · Vercel — AI Gateway · Tech Times — Marvell joins the S&P 500 on Jun 22, 2026 · Intellectia — Chip stocks rebound, June 2026
